revision.text:
<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:大極殿]][[zh:大極殿]]
{{Confuse|Daigokuden|x1=Chinese|qq=}} [[File:HeijokyusekiDaigokuden.jpg|thumb|Restored First Daigokuden of Heijō Palace]] [[File:Daigokuden-ato (Heian Palace)-4.jpg|thumb|250px|{{center|Remains of the Daigokuden in Heian-kyō ({{ill|Kyoto Prefecture|en|Kyoto Prefecture|qid=Q120730|lt=Kyoto|1=Kyoto Prefecture}}, [[:en:Kyoto City|Kyoto City]])|qq=}}{{small|Located in a children's park northwest of Senbon and Marutamachi streets.|qq=}}]] '''Daigokuden''' (大極殿) was the main audience hall of the {{ill|Imperial Court in Kyoto|en|Imperial Court in Kyoto|qid=Q11680900|lt=Imperial Court in Kyoto|1=Imperial Court in Kyoto}} in ancient times. ==Overview== The Daigokuden, located at the northern central edge of the [[Chōdō-in]] within the [[Kyūchū|Imperial Palace]], housed the [[:en:Takamikura|Takamikura]] (Imperial Throne). It served as the site for the [[Sokui-no-rei|Imperial enthronement ceremonies]] and other national rituals. In Chinese [[:en:Daoism|Daoism]], the term refers to the residence of the [[Tianhuang Dadi|Heavenly Emperor]]. The name "Daigokuden" originates from the term {{ill|Taiji (philosophy)|en|Taiji (philosophy)|qid=Q53685|lt=Taiji|1=Taiji (philosophy)}}, meaning the source of all things and the center of the {{ill|Sky|en|Sky|qid=Q527|lt=heavens|1=Sky}}. In China, it was referred to as the [[:en:Taijidian|Taijidian]], originating as a building within walled city compounds. The first Taijidian is said to have been constructed during the [[:en:Three Kingdoms (China)|Three Kingdoms]] period under the [[:en:Wei dynasty|Wei]] Emperor Ming in the 3rd year of Qinglong (235) in [[:en:Luoyang, Henan|Luoyang]]. Thus, the Daigokuden is also read as "Daigyokuden," signifying the center from which the emperor governs the world.<ref>Watanabe (2001) 11. 33–36</ref> There are two theories about the first Japanese palace to house a Daigokuden: the [[Asuka Kiyomihara Palace]] (advocated by Fukuyama Toshio, Ozawa Takeshi, and Watanabe Akihiro)<ref>The Asuka Kiyomihara theory is proposed in Fukuyama Toshio (1984), Ozawa Takeshi (1997), and Watanabe Akihiro (2002).</ref> and the [[:en:Fujiwara Palace|Fujiwara Palace]] (su11orted by Kano Hisashi and Kitō Kiyoshi).<ref>The Fujiwara Palace theory is proposed in Kano Hisashi (1990) and Kitō Kiyoshi (2000).</ref> ==Origins== [[File:Plan of Oharida Palace.jpg|thumb|220px|right|Presumed layout of the Oharida Palace]] The origin of the Daigokuden can be traced back to the "Ōden" (Great Hall) of the [[Oharida Palace]] in {{ill|Asuka, Nara|en|Asuka, Nara|qid=Q752397|lt=Asuka|1=Asuka, Nara}}. Built in {{ill|603|en|603|qid=Q31050|lt=603|1=603}} (the 11th year of {{ill|Empress Suiko|en|Empress Suiko|qid=Q298057|lt=Empress Suiko|1=Empress Suiko}}’s reign), Oharida Palace replaced [[Toyoura Palace]]. According to the ''[[Nihon Shoki]]'', its layout featured a southern main gate (South Gate), northward halls for [[:en:Officials|court officials]], and an open courtyard ([[Chōtei]]) at the center. At the far north stood the Great Gate and the main hall (Ōden) for the empress. This palace structure was described during visits by delegations from {{ill|Sui dynasty|en|Sui dynasty|qid=Q7405|lt=Sui|1=Sui dynasty}} ({{ill|Pei Shiqing|en|Pei Shiqing|qid=Q10516050|lt=Pei Shiqing|1=Pei Shiqing}} in 608) and {{ill|Silla|en|Silla|qid=Q28456|lt=Silla|1=Silla}} (in 611). The hallways ({{ill|Chō|en|Chō|qid=Q1877216|lt=Chō|1=Chō}}) are believed to have been precursors to the later [[Chōdō]] halls. [[Yoshimura Takehiko]] suggested that while simple, Oharida Palace's structure provided the foundation for future Imperial Palaces, such as Fujiwara and Heijō Palaces.<ref name=yoshi1>Yoshimura (1991) 11. 119–120</ref> However, the remains of the main hall and related structures have not yet been discovered, so details about their size remain unknown. ==Further Historical Developments== ===Daigokuden in Fujiwara Palace=== The Daigokuden in [[:en:Fujiwara Palace|Fujiwara Palace]] marked the establishment of formal audience halls in Japan. The palace was designed with reference to Chinese [[:en:city planning|city planning]] and incorporated a [[Jōbō]] grid system. It featured a symmetrical layout centered around the Daigokuden, exemplifying "idealized city planning" based on classical texts like ''[[:en:Zhouli|Zhouli]]'' and ''[[Kōgōki]]''.<ref name=ozawa>Ozawa (2002) 11. 235–257</ref> This configuration became the prototype for later Japanese capitals, such as [[Heijō-kyō]] and {{ill|Heian-kyō|en|Heian-kyō|qid=Q751907|lt=Heian-kyō|1=Heian-kyō}}, integrating ceremonial and governmental functions. ==Notes== <references /> ==Related Topics== * [[Naniwa Palace]] * [[Heijō-kyō]] * {{ill|Heian-kyō|en|Heian-kyō|qid=Q751907|lt=Heian-kyō|1=Heian-kyō}} * [[Chōdō-in]] * [[Daigokuden]] {{architecture-stub|qq=}} {{japanese-history-stub|qq=}}
{{translated page|ja|大極殿|version=102825750|comment=Imported full ja history}}
[[ja:大極殿]]
